text: Three 8mm trocars are positioned along the patient's left side. The surgeon begins creating a flap near the median fat pad, using blunt dissection and focused cautery to carefully develop the plane without tearing the thin peritoneum. The flap is extended laterally to reduce tension and ensure adequate coverage for the mesh placement.

The surgeon uses a heavyweight proline mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, to address the hernia in a patient with higher BMI. The dissection proceeds bluntly across the median fat pad, over the falciform ligament, demonstrating the medial ligaments and teres to a medical student.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection on the contralateral side, lateral to the fat pad, carefully approaching the peritoneum. The dissection is conducted above and below the hernia defect to create a 'volcano sign,' aiding in maintaining the integrity of the peritoneum and preventing posterior holes.

The surgeon dissects down into the hypogastrium, revealing scarring from a previous incision. The multi-lobed hernia defect is visible as the surgeon begins to reduce the hernia contents, utilizing the volcano sign technique.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia contents, successfully preserving the surrounding tissue without creating posterior defects.

The surgeon works on the lateral aspect of the fat pad on the patient's right-hand side, where the peritoneum appears thin. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon navigates into a proper plane, avoiding the creation of holes. Despite a superior attempt resulting in a hole, the inferior approach successfully separates the tissue easily. The surgeon remains posteriorly within the peritoneum, with the transversalis fascia still intact, following the plane like digging a cave.

The surgeon continues to follow the dissection plane laterally and back superiorly, creating small holes in the process. These holes will be closed with a 3-0 Vicryl suture before the procedure concludes. The surgeon aims to create a large pocket for a 15x15 mesh, relying on experience to gauge the size.

The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum laterally, aiming to create a lax flap that extends further to the patient's left beyond the initial flap creation line. The arcuate line is visible during this process.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection posterior to a previously created hole, then moves superiorly above it. The tissue drops down easily as the dissection progresses above the falciform ligament.

The surgeon meticulously maintains hemostasis while performing inferior dissection below the arcuate line. The bare muscle belly of the rectus is visible, as the surgeon creates sufficient space for a 15x15 mesh placement.

The surgeon begins defect closure by reducing the pressure to about eight. The closure starts slightly superior to the hernia defect, which appears bilobed or multi-lobed. The surgeon assesses that the defect aligns better vertically than transversely for closure.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect vertically, pulling the ipsilateral flap closer to the midline. The closure technique visibly reduces the lateral distance from the midline, enhancing the appearance of the surgical field.

The surgeon places a mesh into the hernia defect, securing it with a trio silk suture tied at the midline. The mesh is anchored in the direct midline of the defect, and interrupted sutures are used to secure all four corners. The mesh extends beyond the pocket on the ipsilateral side due to the positioning relative to the camera and lateral dissection.

The surgeon brings the mesh across the midline and ipsilateral start point without tension, using Vicryl sutures to close the holes in the abdomen before closing the flap. The closure is performed from right to left, similar to inguinal hernia repair.

The surgeon carefully extends the flap to the ipsilateral side beyond the edge of the mesh to prevent clamshelling or folding at the corner. The flap is manipulated with caution, especially when moving inferiorly, to avoid inadvertently grasping the epigastric vessels.
